scan_range.scope: Broadened beyond delta — scanned index.astro, why.astro, about/index.astro, evidence/economic-landscape.mdx, gap/arguments.mdx (key site pages not previously covered by delta scans)

findings.description: The statement 'No enforceable framework protects workers displaced by automation' overstates the gap. Existing U.S. programs — unemployment insurance, the WARN Act (Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act, 60-day layoff notice requirement), and Trade Adjustment Assistance — all provide displacement-related protections. The ICESCR's Article 6 right to work goes beyond these programs, but the current phrasing implies no protections exist at all.
findings.suggestion: Qualify the claim: 'No enforceable right-to-work framework protects workers displaced by automation' or 'No enforceable framework guarantees the right to work for workers displaced by automation.' This preserves the ICESCR argument while acknowledging existing (limited) protections. Alternatively, 'No enforceable framework guarantees re-employment for workers displaced by automation' — which distinguishes the right-to-work gap from existing cushioning programs.
findings.context:
No Right to Work

No enforceable framework protects workers displaced by automation. AI may eliminate roles faster than new ones appear...

findings.description: Duplicate heading '## The ICESCR Connection' appears at both line 116 and line 153. Two h2-level sections with identical text in the same page creates ambiguous anchor links (both generate #the-icescr-connection) and confuses screen reader navigation.
findings.suggestion: Rename the second instance to distinguish its purpose. The first (line 116) presents a data table mapping conditions to ICESCR articles. The second (line 153) provides a narrative synthesis. Consider '## Structural Response' or '## Why Binding Framework Matters' for the second instance.
findings.context:
## The ICESCR Connection

The compounding nature of these pressures — trade disruption + conflict costs + AI bifurcation...

findings.description: The researcher variant cites 'Deloitte: 34% deep adoption, 30% limited use, 37% non-adoption' without a hyperlink. The same data on /evidence/economic-landscape links to the Deloitte source URL. The homepage researcher variant should provide the same source access, especially since this audience expects clickable citations.
findings.suggestion: Add the Deloitte link: (Deloitte: 34% deep adoption, 30% limited use, 37% non-adoption)
findings.context: ...bounded by migrating bottlenecks, distributed unevenly across adoption tiers (Deloitte: 34% deep adoption, 30% limited use, 37% non-adoption).

summary.note: Broadened scan scope beyond delta changes to cover high-traffic pages with substantive claims. gap/arguments.mdx and about/index.astro scanned clean — strong fair witness compliance, well-cited, good hedging language throughout. 9 consecutive findings accepted across scans 007–009; this scan covers new page territory.

claims.text: The WARN Act, UI, and TAA provide existing displacement protections that the why.astro statement does not acknowledge.
claims.confidence_basis: WARN Act (29 USC §2101-2109), UI (FUTA), TAA (Trade Act of 1974 §§221-250) — established federal programs. These provide cushioning, not the ICESCR-level right to work.

epistemic_flags: Scan scope broader than prior delta-only scans — covered 5 key pages rather than recent-change files. Coverage remains partial (5 of ~95 content files).

f1 confidence 0.85 — the 'no enforceable framework' statement could be read as shorthand for 'no enforceable right-to-work framework' given the h3 heading context. Fair witness standard still favors explicit precision.
